title: Literature Bridge — Theorems Needed for tan(1) Claim
gap_statement: Prove: no z in EML_1 has arg(z) = -1. Equivalently: no z in EML_1 has Im(z)/Re(z) = -tan(1).

Lindemann_Weierstrass.relevance: e^alpha transcendental for algebraic alpha != 0; pi transcendental

Baker_linear_forms.relevance: Controls log of algebraic numbers; EML_1 values not necessarily algebraic

Nesterenko.relevance: pi, e^pi, Gamma(1/4) algebraically independent; useful once structure known

Gelfond_Schneider.relevance: alpha^beta transcendental for algebraic alpha, beta; tan(1) not of this form

Schanuel_conjecture.relevance: Would show e and e^i algebraically independent; under Schanuel tan(1) not reachable
Schanuel_conjecture.caveat: UNPROVED — one of deepest open problems in transcendence theory

Structural_claim.relevance: If Im(EML_1) can be shown to stay in a set not containing 1
Structural_claim.gap: Im CAN become positive (counterexample: eml(0, y) = -arg(y) > 0 when arg(y) < 0); but 0 not in EML_1

recommended_next_step: Attempt to prove: for all x in EML_1, Re(x) >= 1. If Re(x) >= 1 always, then Im(eml(x,y)) = exp(Re(x))*sin(Im(x)) - arg(y) with exp(Re(x)) >= e ≈ 2.718. Then Im = 1 requires sin(Im(x)) = (1 + arg(y))/exp(Re(x)) = (1 + arg(y))/exp(Re(x)) in [-1/(e-1), (1+pi)/e] = a bounded range. This might be constrainable.
conclusion: No existing proved theorem directly closes the tan(1) gap. Schanuel's conjecture would close it. A structural approach (bounding Re(x) from below) might provide a partial result. Full resolution likely requires new techniques. The claim T_i remains a CONJECTURE supported by: 50,907 computed values (depth <= 5), PSLQ at 300 digits showing no relation, and the self-similar structure reducing to the same claim at each step.
